summary: DeVault describes a C programming pattern used in wlroots that achieves a form of type-safe polymorphism without macros, void pointers, or undefined behavior. The technique uses incomplete struct types that are defined differently by each backend implementation, allowing type-safe function signatures without casting. He walks through the wlr_output example where DRM, Wayland, and X11 backends each provide their own definition of wlr_output_state. However, the post includes an errata noting the approach was rolled back because it causes problems with link-time optimization (LTO).
key_points: Incomplete struct types in C can serve as a limited form of generics by letting each implementation define the struct independently
This pattern eliminates void* casts in backend implementations, improving type safety and readability
The technique was used in wlroots for output backends (DRM, Wayland, X11, RDP) but was later rolled back due to LTO incompatibility
The main limitation is that multiple definitions of the same struct cannot coexist in a single translation unit
money_quotes: This is a pattern I've started using to reduce the number of void* pointers floating around in my code: multiple definitions of a struct.
Nowhere, however, is wlr_output_state defined. It's left an incomplete type throughout all of the common wlr_output code.
The "generic" part is that each output implementation, in its own private headers, defines the wlr_output_state struct for itself.
This allows implementations of the enable, set_mode, and destroy functions to avoid casting a void* to the appropriate type.
The limitations of this approach are apparent: you cannot work with multiple definitions of wlr_output_state in the same file.
We rolled this approach back in wlroots because it causes problems with LTO. I no longer recommend it.
key_insight: C's incomplete struct types can be repurposed as a limited polymorphism mechanism that trades void* unsafety for compile-time type checking, though the technique ultimately breaks under link-time optimization.
